role_summary: SNCC voter-registration worker in McComb, Greenwood, and Hattiesburg, Mississippi; arrested with Fannie Lou Hamer at the Winona bus station in June 1963; Tougaloo College student after the Voting Rights Act.
ai_reading: Simpson's testimony sits inside a tight cluster of Mississippi voter-registration colleagues in the embedding. Counting shared passage-level neighbors across her interview, her six closest are Charles McLaurin (Sunflower County project director, entry 17, count 109), Frankye Adams-Johnson (entry 43, count 99), Juadine Henderson (entry 68, count 75), the joint Ladner sisters interview (entry 75, count 70), Lisa Anderson Todd (entry 79, count 69), and Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96, count 67), every one a Mississippi-side SNCC figure; the embedding confirms her cultural placement inside the Mississippi project network rather than complicating it. On the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle concept axis she reads -0.47 toward the Southern pole, consistent with her geography of work (Greenwood, Itta Bena, McComb, Hattiesburg, Jackson), though that places her in the upper third of the corpus on that axis rather than at its extreme. The reading the cultural record would not have predicted comes from the influence graph: its heaviest who-discusses-whom edges out of Simpson run to Worth W. Long (entry 131, weight 8) and Lawrence Guyot (entry 76, weight 7), the two SNCC field colleagues she names most across the interview, while Fannie Lou Hamer, the figure the historical record binds her to most tightly through the Winona jail night, registers as only a single mentioned edge. The graph weights the organizing network she worked beside daily over the one canonical pairing the public memory of her assigns.
biographical_paragraph: Simpson was born in Itta Bena in the Mississippi Delta, the youngest of seven children, and gives her birth date as December 12, 1945 [src: 1]. She first joined an NAACP youth chapter while living with her sister in Racine, Wisconsin, then returned to Greenwood in January 1963 and joined the SNCC voter-registration movement there at seventeen; the conversation that approved her full-time involvement took place between her father and SNCC field secretary Lawrence Guyot [src: 2]. Working out of the Greenwood Council of Federated Organizations office, she canvassed for voter registration in Greenwood and her home town of Itta Bena, and during the spring of 1964 in Hattiesburg and McComb, then took a shift on the COFO WATS line in the Jackson office during Freedom Summer [src: 2]. The episode that fixed her in the documentary record came in June 1963, when she, Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and Annell Ponder were arrested at the bus station in Winona, Mississippi on the return from a citizenship-school session in South Carolina; held in the Montgomery County jail, Simpson was placed in the cell with Hamer after Hamer was beaten and kept cold water on Hamer's forehead through the night to bring down a fever [src: 2]. She enrolled at Tougaloo College in the fall of 1964 on scholarship funding arranged for the movement workers who wanted to go, alongside Hollis Watkins and MacArthur Cotton, before the Voting Rights Act passed the following year [src: 2]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 41 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: Euvester Simpson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling the Greenwood mass meeting she attended shortly before finishing high school, where she met June Johnson and heard Hollis Watkins and Lawrence Guyot:
interview_snippets.quote: But that night at that meeting, I knew that I had just found exactly what I was looking for, and that was the opportunity to work with people to change the conditions that we lived in.

interview_snippets.speaker: Euvester Simpson
interview_snippets.lead_in: At the Winona bus station in June 1963, after the officers began arresting the group that had used the white waiting room, Fannie Lou Hamer left the bus to ask why:
interview_snippets.quote: Miss Hamer came up, got off the bus and says, what is going on? What are y'all doing? Why are you arresting them? And they threw her in to the car too.

interview_snippets.speaker: Euvester Simpson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Returned to the cell after the jailhouse beatings, with Hamer in pain and running a fever, Simpson had only what the cell held:
interview_snippets.quote: And the only thing I had was a washcloth. And we had a sink there which, you know, I put, kept putting cold water on it. And, you know, and putting it on her forehead trying to bring a fever down.

interview_snippets.speaker: Euvester Simpson
interview_snippets.lead_in: On how the two of them got through the night in the Montgomery County jail cell:
interview_snippets.quote: And so what we did do, I mean, we decided, you know, when she calmed down a bit and the pain wasn't so bad, we decided that we would sing some, some songs.

interview_snippets.speaker: Euvester Simpson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked whether the news of Medgar Evers's assassination, delivered as the group was released, made her want to step back:
interview_snippets.quote: My attitude was that I was just getting started. You know, I'm 17 years old, and I'm home, and this made me more determined to stay involved.

interview_snippets.speaker: Juadine Henderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Juadine Henderson, a teenager visiting Greenwood that week, recalls the group going missing on the return from the citizenship school and Lawrence Guyot being sent to find them:
interview_snippets.quote: June Johnson, who is from Greenwood, [Fannie Lou] Hamer who is from Ruleville, Euvester Simpson, who was from Itta Bena, I think, came up missing. And they sent Lawrence Guyot.

movement_context: The June 9, 1963 Winona, Mississippi Greyhound bus station incident was the precipitating event for the most heavily-documented Black Belt jail beating of the 1962-1965 SNCC Mississippi voter-registration period [src: 3]. Simpson, Fannie Lou Hamer, Annell Ponder, June Johnson, Lawrence Guyot, and James West were returning by Greyhound from a Septima Clark / SCLC citizenship-school training session in Charleston, South Carolina when the bus stopped at the Winona depot in Montgomery County; the group attempted to integrate the white waiting room and the lunch counter, and were arrested by Montgomery County Sheriff Earle Wayne Patridge, Police Chief Thomas Herod Jr., and Mississippi Highway Patrolman John L. Basinger [src: 3]. Inside the Montgomery County jail Ponder, Johnson, and Hamer were beaten by the arresting officers and by two Black prisoners coerced to administer the blows under threat; Simpson, who was seventeen at the time, was placed in the cell with Hamer and kept cold water on Hamer's forehead through the night, and when the pain eased the two of them sang gospel songs quietly in the cell [src: 3]. The injuries Hamer sustained (kidney damage, a permanent limp, partial blindness in her left eye) would shape the rest of her life [src: 4]. The August 22, 1964 Hamer testimony before the Democratic National Convention Credentials Committee in Atlantic City was built around the Winona beating Simpson had witnessed and tended through; Hamer's eight-minute account of the jail violence broke into national prime-time programming when President Lyndon Johnson called a hastily-arranged White House press conference to displace it, and the three television networks aired the testimony in full on their late-evening news programs [src: 4]. The SNCC Mississippi field-staff infrastructure Simpson worked inside (the McComb, Greenwood, Hattiesburg, and Jackson COFO offices) was the same organizing network Charles McLaurin (catalog entry 17) had opened in Sunflower County in August 1962; Simpson's Greenwood and Delta-region voter-registration field work between 1963 and 1965 ran through the same circuit of mass meetings, citizenship classes, and courthouse delegations Bob Moses, McLaurin, Hollis Watkins, and Curtis Hayes had built into the Mississippi project [src: 5].
legacy_and_reception: Simpson's first-person account of the Winona jail beating is the principal eyewitness contribution to the collective-memoir record of the incident, anchoring the relevant chapter in Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner's edited 2010 University of Illinois Press volume Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC (paperback New Press 2014), which Dottie Zellner (catalog entry 30) co-edited [src: 6]. The 2014 SNCC 50th Anniversary Conference at Shaw University in Raleigh that Courtland Cox (catalog entry 24) anchored brought Simpson back into public-facing SNCC alumni programming alongside the Mississippi-project cohort that included Hollis Watkins, Lawrence Guyot, and Charles McLaurin; the conference proceedings and the post-conference SNCC Legacy Project archive at the Robert W. Woodruff Library at the Atlanta University Center include her recorded remarks on the Winona incident and on the Greenwood organizing years [src: 7]. Simpson has continued to live and work in the Mississippi Delta region, with extended periods of residence in Greenwood and the surrounding Leflore and Sunflower County area; her continuing role as a SNCC alumni network speaker has included voter-engagement events at Tougaloo College (her undergraduate institution, where she enrolled in the fall of 1964 on movement-arranged scholarship support) and at the Fannie Lou Hamer Day commemoration the Sunflower County Civil Rights Organization holds each October 6 in Ruleville [src: 7].

gallery.alt: Fannie Lou Hamer at the Democratic National Convention, Atlantic City, August 22, 1964
gallery.caption: Fannie Lou Hamer at the Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City, New Jersey, August 22, 1964, the day she testified before the Credentials Committee about the June 9, 1963 beating in the Montgomery County jail in Winona, Mississippi that Simpson had tended her through. Simpson, seventeen years old at the time of the Winona arrest, was placed in Hamer's cell and kept cold water on her forehead through the night as Hamer recovered from the kidney damage and partial blindness the beating produced. Photographed by Warren K. Leffler for U.S. News & World Report.
gallery.photographer: Warren K. Leffler (U.S. News & World Report)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Library of Congress, U.S. News & World Report collection, digital ID ds.07134)
gallery.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: no known copyright restrictions; U.S. News & World Report dedicated all rights to the public upon donation)

gallery.alt: Tougaloo College Civil Rights Marker, Jackson, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Tougaloo College Civil Rights Marker on the historically Black college campus in Jackson, Mississippi where Simpson enrolled in 1965 with SNCC-arranged scholarship funding after the Voting Rights Act passed. Tougaloo was the institutional base for the Jackson COFO office Simpson worked from during Freedom Summer 1964 and through the Freedom Vote campaign, and continued as the academic home for many SNCC veterans transitioning from full-time field organizing to undergraduate study after 1965.

gallery.alt: Sunflower County Courthouse in Indianola, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Sunflower County Courthouse in Indianola, Mississippi. Simpson's Greenwood and Delta-region voter-registration field work between 1962 and 1965 ran through the same SNCC Mississippi project network that Charles McLaurin (catalog entry 17) opened at this courthouse in August 1962, and the same network that produced the Hamer, Ponder, Johnson, Guyot, and West delegation arrested at the Winona bus station on June 9, 1963.

sources.title: Kay Mills, This Little Light of Mine: The Life of Fannie Lou Hamer
sources.publisher: Plume / Penguin, 1994 (second edition University Press of Kentucky, 2007); WorldCat OCLC 29521716 (the standard biography of Hamer; chapters 6 and 7 document the June 9, 1963 Winona bus station incident, the Montgomery County jail beating, and Simpson's eyewitness presence with Hamer through the night)

sources.title: Maegan Parker Brooks, A Voice That Could Stir an Army: Fannie Lou Hamer and the Rhetoric of the Black Freedom Movement
sources.publisher: University Press of Mississippi, 2014; WorldCat OCLC 856849317 (the principal rhetorical-scholarship account of the August 22, 1964 Hamer testimony before the Democratic National Convention Credentials Committee, including the Winona-beating narrative architecture)

sources.title: Charles M. Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 1995 (second edition 2007); WorldCat OCLC 61664854 (the standard institutional history of SNCC's Mississippi voter-registration project, including the Greenwood, McComb, Hattiesburg, and Sunflower County circuits Simpson worked through)

sources.title: Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner, eds., Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 2010 (paperback New Press 2014); WorldCat OCLC 635021942 (the principal collective-memoir archival document of the SNCC women's cohort; Simpson's first-person account of the Winona jail beating is included)

sources.title: Wesley C. Hogan, Many Minds, One Heart: SNCC's Dream for a New America
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2007 (paperback 2009); WorldCat OCLC 192129687 (institutional history of SNCC documenting the 2014 50th Anniversary Conference at Shaw University and the SNCC Legacy Project archive)

sources.title: Hasan Kwame Jeffries, Bloody Lowndes: Civil Rights and Black Power in Alabama's Black Belt
sources.publisher: New York University Press, 2009; WorldCat OCLC 319497555 (the Black Belt Southern voter-registration institutional history that situates the SNCC Mississippi project Simpson worked alongside the parallel Alabama Black Belt organizing)
